justification: Chapter 4 builds the sustained argument that Mary Magdalene was a foundational, symbolically elevated figure in early Christianity — the Beloved Disciple, eyewitness author of John, and 'apostle to the apostles' honored in Eastern Orthodox tradition. The subsection 'Mary Magdalene as the Fourth Pillar' (lines 500-516) and the surrounding material on Eastern and Byzantine traditions explicitly argue that her high status was preserved outside the Latin West. The Beit She'an mosaic depicting Jesus and Mary Magdalene as sun and moon in the central roundel is new physical evidence (iconographic/archaeological) that directly supports this existing argument: it shows that even within a Jewish-Christian artistic context contemporary with the Johannine community, Mary Magdalene was paired with Jesus as an equal symbolic figure using royal solar-lunar imagery. Chapter 6 contains no discussion of Mary Magdalene and no argument that this evidence could serve — it is concerned with imperial phoenix symbolism, martyrdom literature as resistance narrative, and the political restoration of the Greek East under Constantine. The solar-lunar iconography of the mosaic has no anchor point in chapter 6.
